title: The height you should not set
checkLabels.t8: The strip has no fixed width
checkLabels.t11: The heading is still dark red
why.empty: The editor is empty. Hit the bin and start over.
why.untouched: The code is exactly as it was. The text still spills out of the card.
hint2.untouched: Two lines come out. Nothing goes in.
why.unlinked: The page cannot find styles.css. You changed the link line.
hint2.unlinked: Hit the bin and start over.
why.used-inline: You used style in the tag. That is lesson 01 again.
why.touched-html: You edited the HTML. Here you work only in styles.css.
why.no-card: There is no element with the card class.
why.no-strip: There is no element with the strip class.
why.height-left: There is still a height set.
hint2.height-left: The height of a block comes from its content. Set it and you promise something you cannot keep.
hint3.height-left: Tomorrow the text is two lines longer and it spills past the border. Nobody warns you — it just looks broken.
why.card-width-lost: The card is no longer 320 wide.
hint2.card-width-lost: The width stays. Only the height goes — the two are not the same decision.
why.strip-fixed: The strip still has a fixed width.
hint2.strip-fixed: 900 pixels is wider than a phone screen. And wider than this preview.
hint3.strip-fixed: A block element with no width already takes exactly as much room as it has. It needs no number to do that.
why.padding-cut: You deleted the padding. Only the width goes.
why.h1-broken: The heading is no longer dark red.
blocks.0.text: Two mistakes that look like attention to detail. The card has a height of 120 — read its text in the preview and see where it ends. The strip below has a width of 900 and runs off the screen. Both numbers were written on purpose, by someone who wanted to control the result.
blocks.2.text: A block element grows downwards as much as it needs. That is not a flaw to be fixed — it is the basic behaviour of a page whose content you do not know in advance. Set a height and you freeze the box, and the text simply leaves it. Tomorrow you add a sentence, the day after someone opens the site on a narrower screen and the same text is two lines taller. Nobody complains, nothing crashes — it just looks broken.
blocks.3.text: Setting a height is promising how long the text will be. You cannot promise that.
blocks.5.text: There are cases where the height is known in advance: a round badge, a square photo, an element with a single word in it. There the number is part of the shape, not a guess about content. The badge from lesson 21 is exactly that — 64 by 64, because it has to be a circle.
blocks.6.text: If you want a minimum height without cutting anything off, there is min-height: the box is at least that tall but grows if the content asks. That is almost always the right choice over height.
blocks.8.text: The second mistake is the opposite one. A block element with no width already fills the row available — it needs no number for that. Write 900 and you get a box that does not fit most screens and causes sideways scrolling. So:
blocks.9.items.0: Do not set a width when you want the element to fill the row. It already does.
blocks.9.items.1: Do set one when you want it NARROWER than the row — a card, a column, a page with a readable measure.
blocks.9.items.2: When you do set it, think about narrow screens. max-width: 320px means at most that, and less when there is no room. The number stops being a promise and becomes a ceiling.
blocks.11.text: Remove the card's height so the text can set it. Remove the strip's width so it fits the screen. The padding, borders and the card's width all stay.
